title: MLX: Apple's Native ML Framework
description: MLX is Apple's NumPy-flavoured array framework built natively on unified memory with lazy evaluation. Learn when MLX beats PyTorch+MPS, how mx.eval() works, and the mlx-lm ecosystem for running Llama and Mistral on a Mac.

sections.title: Why MLX?
sections.content:
Coming soon — what MLX is, why Apple built it, and when it beats PyTorch's MPS backend.

<!-- TODO:
  - Lazy evaluation: arrays are computation graphs, only materialized at mx.eval() (or implicitly when their values are needed).
  - Native unified memory: no .to(device) anywhere; arrays just live in RAM, GPU and CPU read the same buffer.
  - NumPy-like API; minimal cognitive switch for ML researchers.
  - When to pick MLX vs. PyTorch+MPS: today MLX often wins on inference for LLMs (mlx-lm); PyTorch wins on training-loop ergonomics and ecosystem (HuggingFace, datasets, accelerate).
-->

sections.title: Lazy Evaluation & mx.eval()
sections.content:
Coming soon — how MLX's lazy graph compilation works in practice and the gotchas it introduces.

<!-- TODO:
  - Code walk: x = mx.random.normal((1000,1000)); y = x @ x; nothing has happened yet.
  - mx.eval(y) forces materialization; printing or .item() also force it.
  - Implications for benchmarking: timer must wrap the eval(), not the graph construction.
  - Compare to torch.compile graph capture and to JAX's lazy/jit semantics.
  - mlx-lm: 4-bit / 8-bit quantization, weight conversion from PyTorch checkpoints.
  - Worked example: port a small transformer block from PyTorch+MPS to MLX, benchmark both.
-->
